one_line_takeaway: A roadmap arguing LLM red teaming should stop chasing abstract single-turn model jailbreaks and instead test realistic, product-grounded, multi-turn threat models and system-level defenses (monitoring, user banning, rapid response, and red-teaming the monitor itself).
key_finding: The central claims are normative rather than measured: (1) model-level robustness is necessary but insufficient because a safe model becomes dangerous once composed with tools, UIs, retrieval, plug-ins, or other models, so the product/system is the correct unit of analysis; (2) single-turn jailbreak defenses do not generalize to multi-turn attacks, so multi-turn should be prioritized; (3) many real harms accrue diffusely over many turns/queries rather than in a single catastrophic response, which means system-level affordances (asynchronous monitoring, banning malicious users, rapid patching of closed- and even open-weight deployments) can mitigate the majority of harm even when model safeguards fail; and (4) reported jailbreak success rates are campaign-dependent and can be gamed (one crafted success = 100% failure rate; 99 added failures = 1%), so scores must be interpreted cautiously and severity-weighted.
core_contribution: A position/roadmap paper (Scale AI) arguing that LLM safeguard red teaming, as currently pursued at NeurIPS/ICLR/ICML, does not prioritize the right problems. It advances two theses: (1) red teaming should target concrete product safety specifications with realistic threat models rather than abstract social biases or ethical principles, and (2) safeguard research should shift from model-level robustness toward system-level safety (monitoring, user detection, rapid response). It introduces a three-layer conceptual framing (model vs. product vs. system) and uses it to reorganize what red teaming should measure. This is an argumentative/agenda-setting contribution, not an empirical study; it proposes no new attack, defense, or benchmark of its own.
threat_model: Rather than assuming one threat model, the paper argues for adopting more realistic ones and criticizes prevailing simplistic setups (e.g., single-turn jailbreaks, unbounded query access, lp-ball perturbations borrowed from vision). Its recommended threat models are product- and deployment-grounded and vary by product type: LLM chatbots (prioritize multi-turn conversational attacks over single-turn; for black-box models assume LIMITED query access because deployers can patch/ban; for white-box models assume the adversary can fine-tune the weights, which typically suffices to strip safeguards); audio assistants (prosody/accent/sarcasm obfuscation, code-switching, background noise, interruptions); video generators (harm that evolves organically across frames, deepfake scams, cross-frame/audio-synchronized harmful content); and autonomous agents (vastly expanded input attack surface across modalities and tool outputs, compounding cross-modal output harms, MCP-server vulnerabilities, and multi-agent miscoordination/agent-vs-agent attacks). At the system level it adds adversarial environments (e.g., indirect prompt injection) and red-teaming the monitor itself (sabotage/evasion of detection).
limitations: By its own framing this is a position paper: it offers no experiments, no new benchmark, and no quantitative validation of its recommendations, so its claims rest on argument and citation of prior work rather than measured evidence. The authors acknowledge the 'delta' problem head-on: red-team environments differ from reality in attacker resources/motivation, in environment fidelity, and in that the model may infer it is being tested and 'play along,' making results hard to interpret. They also note the large engineering lift to build realistic agent sandboxes, and the unsolved normativity problem (defining 'harmful' is context-dependent and lacks consensus). The paper is authored by an industry lab (Scale AI) whose commercial red-teaming interests align with its recommendations, a framing bias worth noting.
